EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropped down the west coast and settled south of the Four Corners to end out 2014. This circulation brought significant snowfall to the lower elevations of southeast Utah near the 4 Corners where 3 to 5 inches fell by the first day of the new year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall of 3 to 4 inches fell over far southeast Utah including the Valley of the Gods.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional moisture moved into Utah in early September, helping to initiate scattered showers and thunderstorms, with some reports of flash flooding on the 5th through 7th. This moisture intensified on the 8th and 9th, as deeper moisture associated with Tropical Storm Norbert moved over the state, producing more widespread flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was widespread across Washington county, including flooding reported in the cities of St. George, Washington, and Ivins. The most common flooding occurred in washes and city streets, with several areas experiencing over a foot of water flowing through the streets. Approximately 15 homes were flooded in the St. George area, and a few homes were damaged in Ivins. Flooding also damaged parts of the Southgate Golf Course in St. George. Many parts of Zion National Park experienced flooding, and the park actually shut down for part of the day when mud, debris, and a large boulder slid onto State Route 9, blocking traffic. The North Fork of the Virgin River rose to 4,790 cfs, the 7th highest recorded flow in the river in the last 100 years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After moisture from Tropical Storm Norbert impacted Utah earlier in the month, more moisture associated with Tropical Storm Odile moved into Utah on September 26 and 27, causing widespread flooding in central and southern Utah, as well as strong gusty thunderstorms in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Seeley burn scar caused a debris flow onto Utah State Route 30, closing the highway for an extended period of time until the debris could be cleared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms increased across Utah on September 20 and 21, with both severe thunderstorms and flash flooding reported during these two days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported in Cannonville, and the hail ripped through a polycarbonate greenhouse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More strong thunderstorms developed between August 18 and August 20, with the strongest thunderstorms moving across the southern portions of Utah. Multiple locations received significant flash flooding from the heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold winter storm moved through Utah in the final week of 2014. The storm brought snow to both the valleys and mountains of northern Utah beginning December 28. As the storm exited the area, it also produced heavy snowfall across far southern Utah, as well as strong downslope winds across portions of northern Utah. Note that this episode continued into early January in far southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall in the Cottonwood Canyons included 23 inches at both Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort and Alta Ski Lifts, as well as 18 inches of new snow at Brighton Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold winter storm moved through Utah in the final week of 2014. The storm brought snow to both the valleys and mountains of northern Utah beginning December 28. As the storm exited the area, it also produced heavy snowfall across far southern Utah, as well as strong downslope winds across portions of northern Utah. Note that this episode continued into early January in far southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall along the northern Wasatch Front included 9 inches at Bountiful Bench, 8 inches in Centerville and Fruit Heights, 7 inches in Farmington, and 6 inches in Clearfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold winter storm moved through Utah in the final week of 2014. The storm brought snow to both the valleys and mountains of northern Utah beginning December 28. As the storm exited the area, it also produced heavy snowfall across far southern Utah, as well as strong downslope winds across portions of northern Utah. Note that this episode continued into early January in far southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong downslope winds developed along the northern Wasatch Front, with peak recorded wind gusts of 78 mph in Farmington, 76 mph at the sensor at US-89 near Lagoon, 69 mph in Centerville, and 59 mph at Bountiful Bench. The winds knocked over a total of three semitrailers on Legacy Parkway and Interstate 15, and some damage to road signs, fences, and trees was also reported, including at least one large tree that blew onto a parked car. Winds also caused power outages across the area, with at least 4,000 customers losing power, mostly in Farmington, Centerville, and Ogden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold winter storm moved through Utah in the final week of 2014. The storm brought snow to both the valleys and mountains of northern Utah beginning December 28. As the storm exited the area, it also produced heavy snowfall across far southern Utah, as well as strong downslope winds across portions of northern Utah. Note that this episode continued into early January in far southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm brought heavy snowfall to the Salt Lake Valley on December 28 and 29, and then the storm was prolonged in the Tooele Valley into December 30 due to the development of additional lake effect snow. Storm total snowfall included 7 inches in Tooele and 6 inches in both Salt Lake City and Cottonwood Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold winter storm moved through Utah in the final week of 2014. The storm brought snow to both the valleys and mountains of northern Utah beginning December 28. As the storm exited the area, it also produced heavy snowfall across far southern Utah, as well as strong downslope winds across portions of northern Utah. Note that this episode continued into early January in far southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty downslope and canyon winds developed in the Salt Lake Valley on the morning of December 30. While these winds were not as strong as further north along the Wasatch Front, wind gusts did reach as strong as 60 mph at the University of Utah. Isolated damage to fences and trees was reported, including one tree as large as 4 feet in diameter that was blown over. In addition, winds caused power outages across the area, with over 4,000 customers in Salt Lake City temporarily losing power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drier than normal conditions continued to plague southeast Utah through the month of November. Precipitation deficits in the vicinity of the 4 Corners ranged from 0.5 to 0.75 of an inch through the month. As a result severe drought conditions remained unchanged in southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained unchanged across far southeast Utah through the month of November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system brought heavy snow to the higher terrain and mountain valleys of northern Utah. The heaviest snowfall occurred during the day of January 9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powder Mountain received 11 inches of new snow. In addition, the Ben Lomond Peak SNOTEL received 1.30 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 18 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system brought heavy snow to the higher terrain and mountain valleys of northern Utah. The heaviest snowfall occurred during the day of January 9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall included 17 inches at Brighton Resort, 12 inches at Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort and Alta Ski Lifts, and 11 inches of snow at Park City Mountain Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Southwest monsoon continued to direct moisture into the Great Basin and central Rockies through September. Climate stations across eastern Utah showed an excess of precipitation of 0.75 to 1.50 inches compared to normal. However only slight improvement of the areal extent of the drought was achieved in the Four Corners area where severe drought conditions persisted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained mainly unchanged across far southeast Utah even though precipitation totals were above normal for September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall during the evening of the 9th and through the day on the 10th caused many normally dry washes to run. A few of the washes ran hard enough to produce flash flooding in Arches and Canyonlands National Parks and adjacent areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding along the washes above and through Arches National Park led to the closure of Wolfe Range Road and Delicate Arch Road in the park for an extended period on September 9th and 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rainfall during the evening of the 9th and through the day on the 10th caused many normally dry washes to run. A few of the washes ran hard enough to produce flash flooding in Arches and Canyonlands National Parks and adjacent areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large section of a county road east of White Rim Road in Canyonlands National Park was underwater during flash flooding, with up to a foot of mud deposited over the roadway. White Rim Road in the park was also flooded which stranding a number of back country campers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved through the Vernal area and produced very strong and damaging outflow winds.
Severe thunderstorms moved through the Vernal area and produced very strong and damaging outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm gusts brought down power lines and snapped off large tree limbs in excess of 6 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding in portions of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The three main washes that run through Arches National Park flowed high with fast moving water and debris on September 28th due to earlier heavy rainfall upstream.  Roads near Delicate Arch were closed that day due to the flash flooding and also due to the mud and debris that was deposited onto the road surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in flash flooding in portions of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flood waters at least four feet deep flowed down the normally dry Bittercreek Wash and swept away a jeep which had been driven into the flood waters where the Tabeguache Trail crosses the wash.  The driver was able to get out of the vehicle and reach the bank while the passenger was trapped inside and drowned.  The vehicle came to a stop about 200 feet downstream from the Tabeguache Trail crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into Utah on Christmas Day, just in time to bring a white Christmas to many residents in Utah's valleys. Significant snowfall also occurred in most mountain locations across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snow totals in the Salt Lake Valley were on the benches, especially early in the event. On the valley floor, many locations received just a trace of snow with the initial cold front early Christmas morning, but steadier snow developed through the day to create higher totals. Storm total snow amounts included 14 inches in Sandy, 13 inches in Cottonwood Heights, 12 inches in Upper Millcreek, 8 inches in Olympus Cove and the Upper Avenues, and 5 to 7 inches in many locations, including most of Salt Lake City proper.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along a 400 ft stretch of Highway 191 where nearly a foot of sand, rocks and debris were deposited on the roadway.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first major winter storm of the season moved into Utah beginning November 22, with precipitation lingering through November 24. The strongest winds occurred early in the event, and precipitation began as rain in the valleys and snow in the mountains. Accumulating snowfall eventually reached the valley floors of northern Utah, but snow depths in the lower elevations were not significant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts were recorded in Utah's northwest desert both pre-frontally and post-frontally, with peak recorded gusts of 69 mph at Wendover Peak, 62 mph at Wendover Airport and Camel Back Mountain, 60 mph at Rosebud and Upper Cedar Mountain, and 59 mph at Promontory Point.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tony Grove Lake SNOTEL received 2.90 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 30 inches of new snow. In addition, winds were strong on the mountain peaks across northern Utah, with a maximum recorded wind gust of 91 mph at Ogden Peak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in northeast Utah creating flash flooding from runoff in the John Jarvie Ranch area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall fell in the Brown's Park basin lead to flash flooding in the John Jarvie Ranch and Red Creek areas. Over ten miles of paved and dirt roads were damaged with many washed out sections.  A coffer dam failed, and water over two feet deep flowed across one road.  The normally small Red Creek became a raging river.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains overnight flooded local drainages and several roadways in the Needles district of Canyonlands National Park.  Some roads were impassable due to deep mud after the flood waters abated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall occurred in eastern Utah which filled the Salt and Winter Camp washes in Arches National Park and washes along the Colorado River valley. In the latter, the runoff was swifter and affected county roadways and a highway bridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding and a mudslide up to a foot deep occurred along Highway 128 near mile marker 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture focused on eastern Utah resulted in heavy rainfall at a rate of one inch per hour which caused flooding and debris flows in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: UDOT reported a flash flood and debris flow up to a foot deep across highway 128 near mile marker 18 where Professor Creek intersects the roadway. The road was closed approximately 2 hours due to flash flooding and the post-flood debris removal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture focused on eastern Utah resulted in heavy rainfall at a rate of one inch per hour which caused flooding and debris flows in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in a residential section of Maeser, with street flooding also reported in nearby areas. Most of the flood damage occurred to landscaping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall occurred in eastern Utah which filled the Salt and Winter Camp washes in Arches National Park and washes along the Colorado River valley. In the latter, the runoff was swifter and affected county roadways and a highway bridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The road to Delicate Arch road was closed late it 1030 pm MDT August 13th through much of the day on the 14th as Salt Valley wash and Winter Camp wash flowed over the road and the nearby parking lot. Local trails were also closed for an extended period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Saturated soil conditions from the previous day of flooding and heavy rain left conditions favorable for rapid rainfall runoff in the Uintah Basin. Thunderstorms in the afternoon of August 20th, again contained heavy rainfall rates in excess of an inch and caused flash flooding in the town of Maeser.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several residences in the western portion of Maeser experienced flash flooding, and street flooding was reported in much of Maeser. A road was also washed out in Dry Fork Canyon to the north of town.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system brought valley rain and mountain snow to the majority of Utah starting November 1. Strong gusty winds blew ahead of and just behind the associated cold front, with the highest gusts across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts from the Great Salt Lake and Utah's northwest desert included 81 mph at Badger Island, 77 mph at Hat Island and Upper Cedar Mountain, 74 mph at Wendover Peak, 73 mph at Camel Back Mountain, and 65 mph at Simpson Springs and English Village in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system brought valley rain and mountain snow to the majority of Utah starting November 1. Strong gusty winds blew ahead of and just behind the associated cold front, with the highest gusts across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across the Salt Lake and Tooele valleys included 76 mph at the Interstate 80 at Milepost 78 sensor, 74 mph at Vernon Hill, and 65 mph at the Great Salt Lake Marina. In addition, these high winds caused problems for high profile vehicles traveling along Interstate 80. Two semitrucks were knocked over by the strong winds, both near Milepost 78. One of the trucks was pulling two trailers, and this accident closed Interstate 80 for a couple of hours. In addition, a pickup truck pulling a trailer also flipped onto its side along the same stretch of Interstate 80. High winds also caused power outages, with nearly 2,000 homes and businesses losing power during the event. Some tree damage was also reported over western portions of the Salt Lake Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system brought valley rain and mountain snow to the majority of Utah starting November 1. Strong gusty winds blew ahead of and just behind the associated cold front, with the highest gusts across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts in west central Utah included 61 mph at Fish Springs and 58 mph in Fillmore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms across southeast Utah caused heavy rainfall which produced flash floods from Arches National Park to near Monticello.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mud and water up to 6 inches deep was reported to be flowing over Highway 191 just north of Monticello.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into Utah on Christmas Day, just in time to bring a white Christmas to many residents in Utah's valleys. Significant snowfall also occurred in most mountain locations across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cedar City received the most snowfall in southwest Utah, with parts of the city reporting 7 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system brought valley rain and mountain snow to the majority of Utah starting November 1. Strong gusty winds blew ahead of and just behind the associated cold front, with the highest gusts across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum recorded wind gusts in southwest Utah included 61 mph at the Milford ASOS and 58 mph at the Cedar City ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first major winter storm of the season moved into Utah beginning November 22, with precipitation lingering through November 24. The strongest winds occurred early in the event, and precipitation began as rain in the valleys and snow in the mountains. Accumulating snowfall eventually reached the valley floors of northern Utah, but snow depths in the lower elevations were not significant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cottonwood Canyons received steady snowfall through much of this event, with Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort, Brighton Resort, and Alta Ski Lifts all measuring a storm total of 30 inches new snow. In addition, Mount Baldy recorded a peak wind gust of 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms across southeast Utah caused heavy rainfall which produced flash floods from Arches National Park to near Monticello.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delicate Arch Road was closed for the day due to fast moving water up to a half foot deep that ran across the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into Utah on Christmas Day, just in time to bring a white Christmas to many residents in Utah's valleys. Significant snowfall also occurred in most mountain locations across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall in the Cache Valley included 7 inches in Richmond and 6 inches in North Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into Utah on Christmas Day, just in time to bring a white Christmas to many residents in Utah's valleys. Significant snowfall also occurred in most mountain locations across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall along the northern Wasatch Front included 12 inches at Bountiful Bench, 10 inches at Centerville, 9 inches at Northeast Ogden Bench and Bountiful, and 5 to 7 inches at many other locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into Utah on Christmas Day, just in time to bring a white Christmas to many residents in Utah's valleys. Significant snowfall also occurred in most mountain locations across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heaviest snowfall in the Wasatch mountain valleys occurred in the Ogden Valley, with 12 inches of new snow reported in Liberty. Heber City also received 7 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into Utah on Christmas Day, just in time to bring a white Christmas to many residents in Utah's valleys. Significant snowfall also occurred in most mountain locations across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Huntington Horse SNOTEL received approximately 19 inches of new snow for the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved into Utah on Christmas Day, just in time to bring a white Christmas to many residents in Utah's valleys. Significant snowfall also occurred in most mountain locations across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parts of west central Utah received significant snowfall with this storm, including 12 inches of snow in Fillmore and 11 inches in Oak City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another storm system moved through Utah where the winds were a bigger impact than the precipitation, with the strongest winds occurring pre-frontally.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts in the Cache Valley included 63 mph at Wellsville and 62 mph at Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm moved through Utah in the beginning of May, bringing heavy snowfall to the higher terrain, as well as some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple hours of strong winds were recorded at the Badger Island sensor, with a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm moved through Utah in the beginning of May, bringing heavy snowfall to the higher terrain, as well as some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were recorded at several locations in the Uinta Mountains, including peak recorded wind gusts of 86 mph at Lofty Lake Peak, 84 mph at Windy Peak, and 76 mph at Dry Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm moved through Utah in the beginning of May, bringing heavy snowfall to the higher terrain, as well as some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ben Lomond Peak SNOTEL received 4.10 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 28 inches of new snow. Several other SNOTEL sites in the area also reported snow totals in the 18-24 inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm moved through Utah in the beginning of May, bringing heavy snowfall to the higher terrain, as well as some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys included 73 mph at the Great Salt Lake Marina, 61 mph at Vernon Hill, 60 mph at a sensor in Sandy, and 58 mph at Stockton Bar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm moved through Utah in the beginning of May, bringing heavy snowfall to the higher terrain, as well as some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milford ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche in the Uinta Mountains of northeastern Utah resulted in the death of one snowmobiler.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two brothers were snowmobiling in the Uinta Mountains, on a popular slope in Gold Hill Basin. One of them, a 39-year-old male, triggered an avalanche, which caught, carried, and buried him several feet deep in snow. The other brother was able to dig him out and attempt CPR, but the victim was pronounced dead at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm began impacting Utah on March 25, bringing increasing winds, followed by showers on March 26. Most of these winds, rain showers, and snow showers did not have any significant impacts, but two isolated events in southern Utah reached severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St. George AWOS recorded a wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another Pacific storm system brought strong, gusty winds to the state of Utah. Gusts greater than 60 mph were recorded in both the northern and southern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sensor in Saratoga Springs recorded strong winds both ahead of and behind the cold front, with a peak recorded gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another Pacific storm system brought strong, gusty winds to the state of Utah. Gusts greater than 60 mph were recorded in both the northern and southern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southwesterly winds occurred in Milford, with a peak recorded gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another Pacific storm system brought strong, gusty winds to the state of Utah. Gusts greater than 60 mph were recorded in both the northern and southern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of strong west-southwesterly winds were recorded at the Bullfrog Marina sensor, with a peak wind gust of 70 mph.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought valley rain and mountain snow to Utah, with strong winds recorded in some of the western valleys on December 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts included 63 mph at Upper Cedar Mountain and 58 mph at Diddle Knoll.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought valley rain and mountain snow to Utah, with strong winds recorded in some of the western valleys on December 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar City ASOS recorded a maximum wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and associated cold front brushed northeast Utah and produced significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 3 to 8 inches were measured across the area, mainly above the 8000 foot level.  Wind gusts of 25 to 50 mph produced widespread blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant rockslide occurred in southeast Utah after a freeze-thaw cycle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall during the months prior to this event resulted in pockets of water in cracks within the sandstone rock formations above the rail line used for the UMTRA superfund hazardous waste removal site just northwest of Moab.  A freeze-thaw cycle occurred just prior to a large section of cliff face which broke off and tumbled down onto the railroad tracks and blocked the north portal of the railroad tunnel.  Some of the boulders also rolled well beyond the railroad tracks and onto a service road used by the UMTRA project trucks.  In addition to a section of railroad track, a gantry crane was also damaged.  It took over a month for rock removal and repairs to be completed.  During that time, most of the hazardous waste removal activities were suspended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and associated cold front brushed northeast Utah and produced significant snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A relatively quick burst of snowfall resulted in accumulations of 1 to 2 inches which made roadways slick and caused numerous vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific trough produced significant to heavy early season snowfall in the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches were measured across the area above the 8000 foot level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific trough produced significant to heavy early season snowfall in the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches were measured across the area above the 7000 foot level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific trough produced significant to heavy early season snowfall in the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5 to 10 inches were measured across the area above the 8000 foot level.  Locally higher amounts included 14 inches at the La Sal Mountain SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of moist weather disturbances moved across Utah during the second week of February, bringing an extended period of snowfall to northern Utah. In addition to the new snow, two avalanche fatalities occurred during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of six snowshoers were traveling just north of Tibble Fork Reservoir in American Fork Canyon when they triggered a small avalanche. One of the snowshoers, a 21-year-old female, was swept away and buried in the snow. While she was able to be resuscitated, she passed away the next day in an area hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of moist weather disturbances moved across Utah during the second week of February, bringing an extended period of snowfall to northern Utah. In addition to the new snow, two avalanche fatalities occurred during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of four snowmobilers were recreating near the Left Fork Huntington Creek when one of them became stuck.  Another person, a 36-year-old male, circled uphill to come help; he triggered an avalanche that buried him but did not catch the stuck snowmobilers. The other snowmobilers began rescue efforts immediately, but the victim was buried 6-8 feet deep, and he passed away at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of moist weather disturbances moved across Utah during the second week of February, bringing an extended period of snowfall to northern Utah. In addition to the new snow, two avalanche fatalities occurred during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall across the Cache Valley included 14.5 inches at Hyde Park, 12.5 inches in Richmond, and 12 inches of new snow at both Utah State University and North Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of moist weather disturbances moved across Utah during the second week of February, bringing an extended period of snowfall to northern Utah. In addition to the new snow, two avalanche fatalities occurred during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Trial Lake SNOTEL received 3.90 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 35 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of moist weather disturbances moved across Utah during the second week of February, bringing an extended period of snowfall to northern Utah. In addition to the new snow, two avalanche fatalities occurred during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much of the precipitation remained as rain in Coalville, and two straight days of rain proved to be too much for one of the open culverts in the town. When that drainage system failed to handle the volume of water, four homes were flooded along 150 South in Coalville, with two of those homes experiencing extensive flooding. One of the significantly damaged homes was flooded with a couple feet of water, and the other had 6 inches of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on February 16, with gusty winds observed both ahead of and behind the front, particularly across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sensor in Saratoga Springs recorded a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on February 16, with gusty winds observed both ahead of and behind the front, particularly across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across far northwest Utah included 63 mph at Promontory Point, 59 mph at the State Route 30 at Curlew sensor, and 58 mph at the Groome sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on February 16, with gusty winds observed both ahead of and behind the front, particularly across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US-40 at Starvation sensor recorded a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system moved through Utah on February 27. In addition to statewide precipitation, the system also helped produce some thunderstorms along the Utah/Arizona border, which caused some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bullfrog Marina sensor recorded several strong wind gusts, reaching a peak speed of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on April 22, bringing gusty, and sometimes destructive, winds to most of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strongest wind gusts in Utah Valley occurred after the cold frontal passage, with peak recorded gusts of 73 mph in Lehi, 66 mph in Saratoga Springs, 65 mph in Highland, and several other gusts in the 50-65 mph range. Several large trees were knocked over by the wind, especially in the Lehi area. In addition, the flagpole in front of the Eagle Mountain city office was blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on April 22, bringing gusty, and sometimes destructive, winds to most of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Interstate 15 at the Sevier River sensor recorded a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on April 22, bringing gusty, and sometimes destructive, winds to most of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts in southwest Utah included 64 mph at the Milford ASOS, 62 mph at Brimstone Reservoir, and 60 mph at the Cedar City ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system brought heavy snow to the higher terrain and mountain valleys of northern Utah. The heaviest snowfall occurred during the day of January 9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the Wasatch mountain valleys, snow was heaviest in the Ogden Valley, with 10 inches of snow in Liberty and 9 inches of snow in Eden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively slow-moving upper-level storm system brought snowfall to Utah, with the heaviest snow isolated to the far northern mountains. In addition, winds were gusty across the state, and led to some damage in Ferron, Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tony Grove Lake SNOTEL received 1.60 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 20 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and moderate southwest flow ahead of an upper low produced significant to heavy snowfall and gusty winds in the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This event which began on the last day of February and continued into March produced snowfall amounts of 5 to 6 inches across the area. Wind gusts of 25 to 40 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist westerly flow with embedded disturbances resulted in areas of heavy snow in the higher elevations of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5 to 10 inches were measured across the area..  Locally higher amounts included 14 inches at the La Sal Mountain upper SNOTEL site.  The snow level was above the 9000 foot elevation until midday on the 15th when it began to lower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively slow-moving upper-level storm system brought snowfall to Utah, with the heaviest snow isolated to the far northern mountains. In addition, winds were gusty across the state, and led to some damage in Ferron, Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 2 pm MST, strong winds in Ferron pulled the roof off of the fire station in town, with only the roof's plywood substructure remaining. The maximum recorded wind gust in Ferron was 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and moderate southwest flow ahead of an upper low produced significant to heavy snowfall and gusty winds in the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 12 to 15 inches were measured in the Abajo Mountains.  Lesser amounts were measured across the remainder of the zone. Wind gusts of 25 to 40 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow.  Locally stronger winds on ridgetops included a gust to 73 mph on La Sal Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist airmass in the beginning of May allowed for spring thunderstorms to form across Utah, with a few of these producing large hail or strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail with a diameter of 0.75 inches was reported near Clarkston, Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist airmass in the beginning of May allowed for spring thunderstorms to form across Utah, with a few of these producing large hail or strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Delta Bus Garage sensor recorded a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist airmass in the beginning of May allowed for spring thunderstorms to form across Utah, with a few of these producing large hail or strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White Reef RAWS recorded a maximum wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and very unstable airmass resulted in isolated strong thunderstorms in northeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hailstones up to the size of ping pong balls fell across sections of the Maeser area.  Minor damage was reported mainly to vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms across Utah became moister beginning July 4, and two separate flash floods occurred in Zion National Park over a three day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Zion National Park, with the river gauge at the North Fork of the Virgin River rising to 253 cfs. Park rangers reported that Behunin Canyon, Heaps Canyon, and Pine Creek all experienced flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms across Utah became moister beginning July 4, and two separate flash floods occurred in Zion National Park over a three day period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred primarily in Pine Creek in Zion National Park. Flows rose to 500 cfs, and the national park recorded the flood on video.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell on the Seeley burn scar on July 10, causing a debris flow in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Seeley burn scar caused a debris flow, with debris and water crossing State Route 31 near Huntington. This debris shut down the highway, and the excessive runoff and debris also damaged a small debris dam located upstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to the state of Utah in the last week of July, with multiple flash flood events reported across the state July 29 through 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Eagle Mountain, Utah, specifically in the Cedar Pass Ranch neighborhood. Flood waters were primarily limited to two properties, with one home experiencing significant damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to the state of Utah in the last week of July, with multiple flash flood events reported across the state July 29 through 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park rangers at Capitol Reef National Park reported flash flooding along Pleasant Creek, the South Draw Road, and the Sheets Gulch area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to the state of Utah in the last week of July, with multiple flash flood events reported across the state July 29 through 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along Sulphur Creek and in the main campground at Capitol Reef National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to the state of Utah in the last week of July, with multiple flash flood events reported across the state July 29 through 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in Croton Canyon near Lake Powell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent moist westerly flow with embedded disturbances occurred across the central intermountain region and produced significant snowfall and blowing snow in the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although were generally less than 5 inches, wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow especially on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent moist westerly flow with embedded disturbances occurred across the central intermountain region and produced significant snowfall and blowing snow in the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 5 to 10 inches were measured across the area. Wind gusts of 20 to 45 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough moved through Utah on July 3, helping to initiate scattered showers and thunderstorms across Utah. However, the lower levels of the atmosphere remained quite dry. This was especially true in northwest Utah, where virga caused strong, gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Callao sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground's mesonet recorded a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Salt Flats sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet registered a maximum wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture increased over Utah beginning July 14, but the air near the surface remained dry, so gusty winds due to thunderstorms were more common than flash flooding on the 14th. On July 15, this moisture became deeper, and combined with a weather disturbance moving through the area to help produce several flash floods across the state of Utah. A single flash flood was reported on July 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 63 mph wind gust was recorded at the I-80 at Grassey, UT sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture increased over Utah beginning July 14, but the air near the surface remained dry, so gusty winds due to thunderstorms were more common than flash flooding on the 14th. On July 15, this moisture became deeper, and combined with a weather disturbance moving through the area to help produce several flash floods across the state of Utah. A single flash flood was reported on July 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flash flood occurred on Utah State Route 12 at Milepost 6. The westbound lane was blocked by debris that was about 4 feet deep and 20 feet long.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms continued across Utah during the second week of August, with several storms producing severe hail and wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts from this thunderstorm included 62 mph at the Salt Lake City International Airport ASOS and 59 mph at the Center Tailings sensor. The winds uprooted trees at the International Center and damaged some buildings and private airplanes at the Salt Lake City International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms continued across Utah during the second week of August, with several storms producing severe hail and wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strong thunderstorm that had moved through Salt Lake and Davis counties, continued into Weber County, with maximum recorded wind gusts of 69 mph at Ogden Peak and 59 mph at the Great Salt Lake Minerals sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional moisture moved into Utah in the first half of June, allowing for increased thunderstorm activity. Most of these were dry thunderstorms, which produced strong, gusty winds, while one storm produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from dime sized to nickel sized was observed in both Bluebell and Myton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large, healthy tree was snapped half way up the trunk after a strong thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture increased over Utah beginning July 14, but the air near the surface remained dry, so gusty winds due to thunderstorms were more common than flash flooding on the 14th. A single flash flood was reported on July 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unseasonably cold storm system brought widespread valley rain and mountain snow to much of Utah, with snow levels dropping to 6000 feet. The biggest impact from this storm was the death of a hiker who was unprepared for the weather conditions. In addition, a funnel cloud was observed near Willard Bay late in the morning on the 17th of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold air funnel cloud was spotted over Willard Bay. The funnel did not move over land.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cold storm system brought snowfall to the mountains of northern Utah, with the heaviest snowfall on Tuesday, June 17. The day before this storm, Monday, June 16, four members of an Ogden family left for a backpacking trip in Wheatgrass Canyon in the back country of Weber County. The family was unprepared for the sudden weather shift, and the father of the family, a 46-year-old male, suffered from cold exposure, as well as potential diabetes complications. Two of the family members hiked out of the canyon on Wednesday to find help, but the man passed away before medical teams could return.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active and moist Southwest monsoon benefited northeast Utah as the main precipitation plume rotated around high pressure centered over the southern High Plains. Southeast Utah, especially near the Four Corners, did not fare as well as northeast Utah even though monthly rainfall totals did end on the positive side of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exteme to severe drought conditions across the Uinta Basin at the beginning of August were reduced to moderate drought to abnormally dry conditions by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to the state of Utah in the last week of July, with multiple flash flood events reported across the state July 29 through 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell on an actively burning fire near Levan and caused a debris flow. Mud and debris flowed onto State Route 28 between Levan and Gunnison, shutting down a 12 mile stretch of the road. Flood waters also washed out a campground, and flooded one basement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain developed across Utah in the first week of August, leading to multiple flash floods across the state. Some flood waters produced significant damage, especially in the city of Helper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Brookside and Veyo, both of which are towns along the Santa Clara River, during the overnight hours. In Brookside, flood waters washed out roads and caused damage to several garages and basements. Near Veyo, a family of 13 was camping in what had been a dry streambed, when flood waters rose and forced them to higher ground. Tents and other camping equipment were washed downstream, and the waters even picked up their minivan, slamming it into a boulder. All 13 people were rescued, with no injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utah State Route 132 was temporarily closed at Milepost 37 due to flooding and debris on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain developed across Utah in the first week of August, leading to multiple flash floods across the state. Some flood waters produced significant damage, especially in the city of Helper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large debris flow occurred in Huntington Canyon and flowed onto State Route 31 after heavy rain fell on the Seeley burn scar. Many boulders and trees flowed onto the roadway, with some trees as large as 40 feet in length and 4 feet in diameter. |The area with the heaviest impact was the North Hughes drainage, with significant flooding and debris also occurring in Cabin Canyon and the Old Folks Flat area. It took the Utah Department of Transportation about 24 hours to reopen the roadway to traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm with rotation produced a funnel cloud in northeast Utah.

An elevated ridgeline existed between the funnel and the eyewitnesses, such that the base of the funnel cloud extended below the ridgeline which prevented any determination of how close the rotation may have gotten to the ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Capitol Reef National Park, including along Pleasant Creek, Sheets Gulch, and Cottonwood Wash. In addition, the Notom Road was impacted by the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and slow moving Pacific trough with an associated strong cold front brought snow to much of eastern Utah during the Christmas Holiday time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Parks of southeast Utah received around 2 inches of new snowfall which was the first significant snow of the season for this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain developed across Utah in the first week of August, leading to multiple flash floods across the state. Some flood waters produced significant damage, especially in the city of Helper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding occurred across Carbon County, with damaging floods reported in Helper, Price, Carbonville, Kenilworth, Spring Glen, and Westwood. Damage to public infrastructure totaled $500,000, including damage to bridges, culverts, and roads. Over 100 homes received at least some flood damage, with most of the flooding occurring in basements, yards, driveways, and garages. Damages to these homes totaled in excess of $2 million.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved through Utah on October 12, bringing valley rain and high mountain snow to much of the state. Gusty winds were observed ahead of and just behind the cold front, with the strongest winds along the mountain peaks and in Utah's northwest desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were strong for several hours across Utah's west desert, and the strongest winds were post-frontal northwesterly winds. Peak recorded gusts included 65 mph at Wendover Peak, 62 mph at Diddle Knoll in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet, and 61 mph at Promontory Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After moisture from Tropical Storm Norbert impacted Utah earlier in the month, more moisture associated with Tropical Storm Odile moved into Utah on September 26 and 27, causing widespread flooding in central and southern Utah, as well as strong gusty thunderstorms in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flash flooding once again occurred in Carbon County, with significant flooding reported in Carbonville, Helper, Kenilworth, Price, and Wellington. The hardest hit area was the Piling's Trailer Court, where at least 24 homes were damaged or destroyed. In total across the county, 53 homes were damaged, including 22 that were declared completely destroyed. In addition, a sewage treatment plant was shut down when it was inundated with 4 feet of water and leaked into the Price River; the damage estimate for the treatment plant was $75,000. Street flooding was widespread across the area, and the Price River overflowed its banks at multiple locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After moisture from Tropical Storm Norbert impacted Utah earlier in the month, more moisture associated with Tropical Storm Odile moved into Utah on September 26 and 27, causing widespread flooding in central and southern Utah, as well as strong gusty thunderstorms in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph wind gust was recorded by the sensor at Interstate 80 at Milepost 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After moisture from Tropical Storm Norbert impacted Utah earlier in the month, more moisture associated with Tropical Storm Odile moved into Utah on September 26 and 27, causing widespread flooding in central and southern Utah, as well as strong gusty thunderstorms in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was again widespread across Washington County, with floods reported in Enterprise, Ivins, Santa Clara, St. George, and Springdale, among others. Most rivers and washes in the area experienced flooding, with particularly high flows in the Beaver Dam Wash and the Virgin River. Rock and mud slides were common across the area, temporarily closing several highways across the county. Flooding also occurred across much of Zion National Park, with the park closing for a period of time due to flooding and debris on roadways. This flooding included in the popular Narrows section of the park, where two hikers were stranded by the flood waters; one of the hikers escaped to safety, but the other, a 34-year-old male, died in the flood. ||In Enterprise, many roadways flooded, and a few businesses were damaged by flood waters. In Ivins, a few homes near 600 S and 400 W experienced flooding. In St. George, damage was most severe at the Southgate Golf Course, with the majority of holes inundated with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area and was followed by the coldest air of the season so far.  Snow fell along and behind the front.  The snow level dropped to near the 8000 foot level.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were measured across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After moisture from Tropical Storm Norbert impacted Utah earlier in the month, more moisture associated with Tropical Storm Odile moved into Utah on September 26 and 27, causing widespread flooding in central and southern Utah, as well as strong gusty thunderstorms in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to heavy rainfall caused flash flooding along the Paria River, especially near Kodachrome Basin State Park. The flood waters undercut the road into the state park, and caused a bridge over the river to collapse. When this occurred, a couple visiting from Italy was crossing the bridge, and their car fell into the river. The car was carried about 150 yards downstream before flipping onto its top. The couple, a 47-year-old female and a 62-year-old male, managed to escape out of a back window before being rescued. Both were taken to the hospital to be treated for hypothermia. The loss of the bridge caused most of Kodachrome Basin State Park to remain closed for an extended period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought cold air at the surface which combined with clear skies and dry air that resulted in a hard freeze overnight in some valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures ranged from 25 to 32 degrees Fahrenheit across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the area and then skies cleared caused freezing temperatures across the zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped into the 20 to 26 degree Fahrenheit range throughout the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system moved though the eastern Great Basin to the Rockies on May 11th. Deep moisture resulted in heavy rainfall in the southeast Utah valleys and  snowfall in the mountains. The heavy rainfall caused flooding in several favored areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park service personnel reported flash flooding along Salt Wash which inundated roads and park attraction sites through much of the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system moved though the eastern Great Basin to the Rockies on May 11th. Deep moisture resulted in heavy rainfall in the southeast Utah valleys and  snowfall in the mountains. The heavy rainfall caused flooding in several favored areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along low lying areas of Highway 211.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system moved though the eastern Great Basin to the Rockies on May 11th. Deep moisture resulted in heavy rainfall in the southeast Utah valleys and  snowfall in the mountains. The heavy rainfall caused flooding in several favored areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall amounts across Canyonlands National Park led to widespread flooding throughout the Needles District portion of the park. The flooding caused several secondary roads to be washed out and closed. Search and rescue operations were needed as well.  Rainfall amounts near the flooding area were around one inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific cold front brought gusty winds to Utah, with the strongest winds in Utah's northwest desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were strong across much of the west desert for a few hours, with more than a dozen sensors recording wind gusts greater than 58 mph. Peak recorded gusts included 68 mph at Wendover Peak and 66 mph at the Interstate 80 sensor in the Dugway mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system moved through Utah on February 27. In addition to statewide precipitation, the system also helped produce some thunderstorms along the Utah/Arizona border, which caused some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60-year-old tree was knocked over in St. George by gusty winds. The tree fell onto the home, causing damage to the roof. There were no injuries associated with the event, as no one was home at the time. The peak recorded wind gust in the area during this time was 49 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of moist weather disturbances moved across Utah during the second week of February, bringing an extended period of snowfall to northern Utah. In addition to the new snow, two avalanche fatalities occurred during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall in the valleys of the Wasatch Mountains included 16 inches in Eden and 10 inches at Deer Creek Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of moist weather disturbances moved across Utah during the second week of February, bringing an extended period of snowfall to northern Utah. In addition to the new snow, two avalanche fatalities occurred during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powder Mountain received a storm total of 36 inches new snow, while Snowbasin Resort received 28 inches of snow. Water totals in the northern Wasatch Mountains were very high; the highest was at the Tony Grove Lake SNOTEL, which reported liquid equivalent precipitation of 7.70 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of moist weather disturbances moved across Utah during the second week of February, bringing an extended period of snowfall to northern Utah. In addition to the new snow, two avalanche fatalities occurred during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall included 47 inches at Brighton Resort, 32 inches at Provo Canyon, and 28 inches at Alta Ski Lifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another moist and slow-moving Pacific storm system brought heavy snow to the mountains of far northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tony Grove Lake SNOTEL received 4.60 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 30 inches of new snow during the warm, but moist, event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another Pacific storm system brought strong, gusty winds to the state of Utah. Gusts greater than 60 mph were recorded in both the northern and southern portions of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys included 65 mph at the Great Salt Lake Marina, 64 mph at Vernon Hill, 61 mph at Flight Park South, and 57 mph at both South Mountain and Olympus Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated, windy thunderstorm in southeast Utah caused some damage on Lake Powell. In addition, a thunderstorm producing heavy rain caused a flash flood at Capitol Reef National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A windy thunderstorm moved over Lake Powell, causing a houseboat to be blown into a cliff. One sensor nearby at Bullfrog Marina recorded a peak wind gust of 39 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated, windy thunderstorm in southeast Utah caused some damage on Lake Powell. In addition, a thunderstorm producing heavy rain caused a flash flood at Capitol Reef National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A visitor to Capitol Reef National Park reported a flash flood in the Cottonwood Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and slow moving upper low produced heavy snowfall to many mountain areas in eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 18 inches were measured across the area.  Locally higher amounts included 26 inches at the Hickerson Park SNOTEL site.  Wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow, with snow drifts up to 3 feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and slow moving upper low produced heavy snowfall to many mountain areas in eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6 to 11 inches were measured across the area. Wind gusts of 25 to 35 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and slow moving upper low produced heavy snowfall to many mountain areas in eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches were measured across the area above the 7500 foot level.  Locally higher amounts included 8 inches in the La Sal Mountains. Wind gusts of 20 to 35 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on April 22, bringing gusty, and sometimes destructive, winds to most of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across west central Utah included 78 mph at the U.S. 6 at Eureka sensor, 73 mph at Fish Springs, and 61 mph at Fillmore Municipal Airport.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on April 22, bringing gusty, and sometimes destructive, winds to most of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across Utah's northwest desert included 82 mph at Wendover Peak, 76 mph at Camel Back Mountain and Upper Cedar Mountain, 74 mph at Diddle Knoll, 72 mph along Interstate 80 at Milepost 1, and many other wind gusts in the 60-70 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on April 22, bringing gusty, and sometimes destructive, winds to most of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts from the Cache Valley included 64 mph at both Lewiston and at the Logan ASOS. Large tree limbs and branches were knocked down across most of the valley, with some downed branches causing power outages in several cities, as well as blocking some roadways. Lewiston Elementary School sustained some minor cosmetic damage from the wind. A warehouse in Logan had its roof blown completely off, and several other roofs sustained damage. Several barns were also blown over, including one in Richmond near Pepperidge Farm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on April 22, bringing gusty, and sometimes destructive, winds to most of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts across the northern Wasatch Front included 72 mph at the I-15 at Tremonton sensor, 66 mph along I-15 at the Willard Point of Entry, 63 mph at Antelope Island, 61 mph at both Brigham City and Plymouth, and 60 mph at Bothwell. The strongest winds near Tremonton toppled two large semitrailer trucks, seriously injuring one of the two drivers. Many large trees were also knocked over across the area, and the wind sent several trampolines flying out of backyards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on April 22, bringing gusty, and sometimes destructive, winds to most of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two sensors in the Heber City area recorded particularly strong wind gusts, with a 63 mph gust at Heber City Municipal Airport - Russ McDonald Field, and 58 mph at the US-40 at Heber sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Utah on April 22, bringing gusty, and sometimes destructive, winds to most of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys included 86 mph at Vernon Hill, 82 mph at South Mountain, 72 mph in Draper, 67 mph at the Great Salt Lake Marina and University of Utah, and several other locations with gusts in the 55-65 mph range. At a construction site in Murray, an eight-story scaffolding collapsed, damaging some of the building material it was holding. At the same site, large marble panels, weighing 80 to 100 pounds each, were picked up by wind and shattered into sharp shards. The winds also damaged several boats at the Great Salt Lake Marina. Many large trees were uprooted, with trees and branches causing power outages when they fell on power lines. A few power poles were knocked over by the winds, and a few power poles also caught fire as a result of the wind and dust brought by the storm. Roof damage was reported in several locations, and roofs were completely blown off of outbuildings in Vernon and Grantsville. Trampolines, fences, and garage doors were also commonly damaged objects in this storm. The wind was blamed for several traffic accidents across the area, both due to low visibilities in blowing dust, as well as due to tree branches falling onto roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving cold front moved through Utah in mid-March, producing strong, and sometimes destructive, winds across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds knocked over two hay barns in Lewiston, Utah. Both barns were 30 feet wide; one was 80 feet long and the other was 100 feet long. The barns were used to store hay, and the toppled hay buried about 20 calves, though none were injured. In addition to the barns, an empty semi-trailer in a parking lot in Logan was knocked over by the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific trough moved across the region in two segments which produced an extended period of light snowfall in the Uinta Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 3 to 6 inches were measured across the area.  Wind gusts of 20 to 30 mph produced localized blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving cold front moved through Utah in mid-March, producing strong, and sometimes destructive, winds across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Antelope Island sensor recorded a peak wind gust of 58 mph. In addition, some damage to shingles and siding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving cold front moved through Utah in mid-March, producing strong, and sometimes destructive, winds across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts across the Salt Lake and Tooele Valleys included 81 mph at Flight Park South, 70 mph at Vernon Hill, 67 mph in Grantsville, 63 mph at Point of the Mountain, and several other reports greater than 50 mph. Shingles were blown off of homes and other roof damage was reported in several locations, especially in the Magna and Grantsville areas. In addition, multiple large trees and traffic light poles were knocked down across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving cold front moved through Utah in mid-March, producing strong, and sometimes destructive, winds across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts included 73 mph in Saratoga Springs and 60 mph recorded in Pleasant Grove, Orem, and at Pioneer Crossing. Damage across the area included multiple fences knocked down by the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and moderate southwest flow ahead of an upper low produced significant to heavy snowfall and gusty winds in the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This event which began on the last day of February and continued into March produced snowfall amounts of 5 to 8 inches across the area. Wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving cold front moved through Utah in mid-March, producing strong, and sometimes destructive, winds across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US-40 at Starvation sensor recorded a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving cold front moved through Utah in mid-March, producing strong, and sometimes destructive, winds across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts across the area included 97 mph on Mount Baldy, and many other gusts in the 50 to 65 mph range. Ski lifts were shut down at multiple mountain resorts, and the strong winds knocked down several large trees in Little Cottonwood Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving cold front moved through Utah in mid-March, producing strong, and sometimes destructive, winds across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in Emery County caused damage in several locations from Huntington to Ferron. Damages included many fences that were blown down, multiple traffic signs bent or broken, and significant damage to at least one roof. In addition, at least one parked trailer was flipped over, and multiple power transmission lines were damaged, resulting in a power outage for around 2,500 people. The top recorded wind gust in this part of the state was 54 mph at Carbon County Regional Airport/Buck Davis Field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm began impacting Utah on March 25, bringing increasing winds, followed by showers on March 26. Most of these winds, rain showers, and snow showers did not have any significant impacts, but two isolated events in southern Utah reached severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trampoline was blown out of a backyard and into some power lines in Minersville. Recorded wind gusts in the area at the time were relatively light, so it is likely that this was caused by a strong dust devil. Time listed is approximate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving cold front moved through Utah in mid-March, producing strong, and sometimes destructive, winds across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts across the area included 59 mph at the Delta Bus Garage and 58 mph at Fish Springs in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving cold front moved through Utah in mid-March, producing strong, and sometimes destructive, winds across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts across the area included 68 mph at the Snowbasin-Straw Top sensor and 67 mph at the Red Spur RAWS. In addition, all nine lifts at Snowbasin Resort were shut down due to the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough produced significant snowfall in the mountains of eastern Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific storm system brought widespread rain and snow to Utah, with the heaviest precipitation in the southern half of the state. Snow levels fell to the valley floors in some locations, with the wet, heavy snow causing damage and creating hazardous driving conditions. Strong winds were also observed in both northern and southern Utah with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the northern edge of the storm system, northeasterly downslope winds developed across the northern Wasatch Front. Multiple wind gusts of 58 mph were recorded at a sensor in Farmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific storm system brought widespread rain and snow to Utah, with the heaviest precipitation in the southern half of the state. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific storm system brought widespread rain and snow to Utah, with the heaviest precipitation in the southern half of the state. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong winds and heavy snowfall caused hazardous travel conditions in west central Utah. In Delta, the Bus Garage sensor recorded a maximum wind gust of 62 mph. In Fillmore, 5.2 inches of snow was recorded for the entire storm. On Interstate 15 near Cove Fort, multiple automobile accidents were reported in the early afternoon of May 11, including a rollover accident, a four-car accident, and multiple trucks that got stuck in the snow on the interstate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific storm system brought widespread rain and snow to Utah, with the heaviest precipitation in the southern half of the state. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pine Creek SNOTEL received 2.70 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 27 inches of new snow.

CZ_NAME: UTAHS DIXIE AND ZION NATIONAL PARK
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific storm system brought widespread rain and snow to Utah, with the heaviest precipitation in the southern half of the state. Snow levels fell to the valley floors in some locations, with the wet, heavy snow causing damage and creating hazardous driving conditions. Strong winds were also observed in both northern and southern Utah with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northeasterly winds developed in Utah's Dixie, with the White Reef RAWS recording a peak wind gust of 73 mph. In addition, several trampolines were blown from yards, and some fence and roof damage was reported. A few power lines were knocked down by the winds, and nearly 4,000 people lost power in several cities across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific storm system brought widespread rain and snow to Utah, with the heaviest precipitation in the southern half of the state. Snow levels fell to the valley floors in some locations, with the wet, heavy snow causing damage and creating hazardous driving conditions. Strong winds were also observed in both northern and southern Utah with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yankee Reservoir SNOTEL received 2.40 inches liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 24 inches of new snow. Travel was impaired due to snowfall along several routes in the southern mountains, including State Routes 14 and 20. In addition, State Route 143 east of Brian Head and State Route 148 over Cedar Breaks were closed for the day due to accumulating snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific storm system brought widespread rain and snow to Utah, with the heaviest precipitation in the southern half of the state. Snow levels fell to the valley floors in some locations, with the wet, heavy snow causing damage and creating hazardous driving conditions. Strong winds were also observed in both northern and southern Utah with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall in southwest Utah triggered many automobile accidents, knocked out power to thousands, and caused widespread tree damage. Cedar City received 12 inches of new snow, and reports of 5-10 inches of snow were common across the area. Dozens of large tree limbs were knocked down, primarily in the Cedar City area, and the tree damage was multiplied by the fact that leaves had already grown in most locations by early May. Travel was greatly impacted by the snowfall, especially along the Interstate 15 corridor between Beaver and Cedar City, where the Utah Highway Patrol reported 21 crashes, 19 slide-offs, and a semitruck rollover in an eight hour period on May 11. Power outages were widespread, and as many as 7,000 people had their power knocked out, primarily due to snow-laden limbs falling into power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific storm system brought widespread rain and snow to Utah, with the heaviest precipitation in the southern half of the state. Snow levels fell to the valley floors in some locations, with the wet, heavy snow causing damage and creating hazardous driving conditions. Strong winds were also observed in both northern and southern Utah with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hole-In-Rock SNOTEL recorded 1.50 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, or approximately 18 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and moist Pacific storm system brought widespread rain and snow to Utah, with the heaviest precipitation in the southern half of the state. Snow levels fell to the valley floors in some locations, with the wet, heavy snow causing damage and creating hazardous driving conditions. Strong winds were also observed in both northern and southern Utah with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong easterly winds developed in the canyons along the Wasatch Mountains on the morning of May 11, especially in Little Cottonwood Canyon. Wind gusts of 72 mph were recorded at the peak of Mount Baldy, and 58 mph wind gusts were recorded at the base of Alta Ski Lifts. Near Alta Ski Lifts and Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort, multiple large trees were knocked down by these winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system passed through Utah beginning April 25. The primary threat from this storm was gusty winds in the southern half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak recorded wind gusts across west central Utah included 66 mph at the Delta Municipal Airport and 58 mph at the U.S. 6 at Eureka sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system passed through Utah beginning April 25. The primary threat from this storm was gusty winds in the southern half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak recorded wind gust across southwest Utah was 61 mph at the Milford ASOS. In addition, these strong winds caused low visibilities in blowing dust. On Interstate 15 between Parowan and Beaver, this led to accidents involving 13 vehicles, resulting in multiple injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system passed through Utah beginning April 25. The primary threat from this storm was gusty winds in the southern half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began just north of Cedar City and was fueled by strong winds, before being contained after burning 45 acres. A landowner had been doing a controlled burn on April 24, and thought had put it out, but some of the embers reignited the blaze with the strong winds and got out of control on April 25. The fire threatened, but did not reach, 30 homes in the area. The wildfire consumed three power poles and caused a power outage; Rocky Mountain Power estimated the damage at $15,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across northern Utah in the early morning hours of April 13, and then continued across much of the state through that afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions began to creep into the Four Corners region by late April as winter and early spring precipitation remained below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a somewhat cool, wet spring, severe drought conditions persisted across extreme southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions remained unchanged across southeast Utah even though precipitation amounts ended up above normal for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 2014 lived up to its reputation as being one of the driest months of the year in southeast Utah.  Precipitation amounts at climate stations in southeast Utah were a third to three quarters of an inch deficit for the month, which allowed severe drought conditions to persist.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below normal precipitation across southeast Utah allowed severe drought conditions to persist across extreme southeast Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist storm system moved through Utah at the end of January, bringing widespread snowfall to the state. The heaviest snowfall was mostly recorded in central Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist storm system moved through Utah at the end of January, bringing widespread snowfall to the state. The heaviest snowfall was mostly recorded in central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sanpete and Sevier Valleys received the most valley snowfall across the state, with storm total snowfall of 10 inches in Manti, 10 inches in Richfield, and 9 inches in Gunnison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist storm system moved through Utah at the end of January, bringing widespread snowfall to the state. The heaviest snowfall was mostly recorded in central Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent moist westerly flow with embedded disturbances occurred across the central intermountain region and produced significant snowfall and blowing snow in the mountains of eastern Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the region causing a season ending freeze for a few valleys in eastern Utah and the Grand Valley in western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped into the 24 to 32 degree Fahrenheit range throughout the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the region causing a season ending freeze for a few valleys in eastern Utah and the Grand Valley in western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped into the 23 to 32 degree Fahrenheit range throughout the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture continued to produce thunderstorms with heavy rainfall in east-central Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in a flash flood which washed out a 50 foot section of railroad track.  A rain gauge near the flash flood measured nearly an inch of rain in less than one hour.  The width of the flash flood was about 400 feet wide where the flood waters reached the railroad tracks. A storm water retention pond with a capacity of about 10 million gallons was completely filled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropped down the west coast and settled south of the Four Corners to end out 2014. This circulation brought significant snowfall to portions of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts around 3 inches were measured across much of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropped down the west coast and settled south of the Four Corners to end out 2014. This circulation brought significant snowfall to portions of southeast Utah.
